result: I keep thinking about the way light pools on my kitchen floor at 4:17 p.m., a gold so specific it feels like the universe is showing off. The kettle clicks off, the cat flicks her tail against the chair leg, and for three unearned seconds everything is mathematically perfect. I stand there holding a chipped mug, suddenly aware that I am the same age my mother was when she had the miscarriage she never mentioned until last year. Time folds like cheap origami; the tiles under my socks are suddenly 1989, the linoleum of a house we sold when I was eight. I smell the old spice cupboard—cinnamon tinged with bus exhaust drifting through the screen door. My father hums off-key behind the newspaper. None of them are here anymore, yet here they are, packed inside a trapezoid of light that will vanish by 4:23. I try to photograph it, but the phone insists on correcting the warmth, turning memory into advertisement. So I just breathe, let the particles land on my arms like harmless ash, and promise to keep this replica of a day safe somewhere behind the eyes, where moisture and metaphor conspire to preserve what the cloud never could. The cat yawns, the moment cools, dishes wait. Outside, the neighbor starts his lawnmower, engine coughing adulthood into the otherwise unmarred afternoon. I sip tepid water, taste rust, decide it’s enough.
